content.problemStatement:
You are given an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​], consisting of NNN integers. In one move, you can take two adjacent numbers AiA_iAi​ and Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​, delete them, and then insert the number Ai∧Ai+1A_i \land A_{i+1}Ai​∧Ai+1​ at the deleted position. Here, ∧\land∧ denotes bitwise AND. Note that after this operation, the length of the array decreases by one.

Formally, as long as ∣A∣>1|A| \gt 1∣A∣>1 (where ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ denotes the current length of AAA), you can pick an index 1≤i<∣A∣1 \leq i \lt |A|1≤i<∣A∣ and transform AAA into [A1,A2,…,Ai−1,Ai∧Ai+1,Ai+2,…,A∣A∣][A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{i-1}, A_i \land A_{i+1}, A_{i+2}, \ldots, A_{|A|}][A1​,A2​,…,Ai−1​,Ai​∧Ai+1​,Ai+2​,…,A∣A∣​].

Find the **minimum** number of moves required to make all numbers in the resulting array equal.


Input Format

The first line of input contains an integer TTT — the number of test cases you need to solve.
The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the size of the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,…,ANA_1, \ldots, A_NA1​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of moves required to make all numbers equal.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^6). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (0 ≤ Ai < 2^30).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of moves required to make all numbers in the array equal.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we compute the total AND G and greedily cut segments when the running AND becomes G. The number of cuts gives the maximal number of equal final elements, and the answer is N minus that count.
Total AND G = 5. Every single element already equals G, so we can cut after each element, obtaining 5 segments. Moves = 5‑5 = 0.
G = 0. Each element yields cur == G immediately, giving 4 segments. No moves are needed.

solution.approach: Let G be the bitwise AND of the whole array. Any sequence of operations can only turn bits off, therefore the final equal value must be G. The array after all operations is a partition of the original array into contiguous segments, each reduced to the AND of its elements. To minimise the number of moves we need to maximise the number of segments whose AND equals G, because each segment contributes one final element and each merge reduces the length by one. Since the AND of a growing segment never increases, we can greedily scan from left to right, maintaining the current segment AND. Whenever this AND becomes exactly G we close the segment, increase the count and start a new segment. This yields the maximum possible number of segments. The answer is N – (number of segments). The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    ALL = (1 << 30) - 1
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        G = ALL
        for v in arr:
            G &= v
        cur = ALL
        segments = 0
        for v in arr:
            cur &= v
            if cur == G:
                segments += 1
                cur = ALL
        out_lines.append(str(N - segments))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
